// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance TemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Heterogeneous sensor measuring temperature parameters with spatial distribution optimized for coverage of physical assets. Supports real-time data transmission via standardized industrial protocols."
    DTDFVocab:producedData TemperatureDataTransmitted
]

instance PressureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor measuring pressure metrics with configurable sampling frequencies aligned with system dynamics. Supports event-triggered and time-based data transmission."
    DTDFVocab:producedData PressureDataTransmitted
]

instance VibrationSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded sensor capturing vibration data for mechanical health monitoring. Implements preprocessing steps including Kalman filtering for noisy data."
    DTDFVocab:producedData VibrationDataTransmitted
]

instance PositionSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor providing positional data for spatial-temporal system representation. Supports integration with 3D/4D visualization models."
    DTDFVocab:producedData PositionDataTransmitted
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance TemperatureDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TemperatureSensor
]

instance PressureDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PressureSensor
]

instance VibrationDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom VibrationSensor
]

instance PositionDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PositionSensor
]

instance ContextualDataTraces : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TemperatureSensor, PressureSensor, VibrationSensor, PositionSensor
    base:desc "Aggregated one-way data flow from physical assets including sensor readings, operational metrics, and environmental parameters for digital shadow purposes."
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance SysMLFunctionalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML-based functional architecture model capturing requirements, use cases, and internal block diagrams (IBDs) for component-connector composition."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLEnabler, MontiArcEnabler
]

instance MontiArcBehavioralModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "MontiArc automaton-based behavioral model defining system states, transitions, and event handling for dynamic behavior simulation."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MontiArcEnabler, SimulinkEnabler
]

instance SimulinkDynamicModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink model implementing differential equations, control systems, and real-time execution of physical system dynamics."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkEnabler, CppEnabler
]

instance StateMachineModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML v1.7 state machine model defining system operational states and failure modes for predictive analytics."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLEnabler, PredictionEnabler
]

instance TemperatureData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Structured temperature data traces aggregated from physical sensors with preprocessing including filtering and normalization."
    DTDFVocab:fromData TemperatureDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler, VisualizationEnabler
]

instance PressureData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Processed pressure data including derived metrics for performance analysis and anomaly detection."
    DTDFVocab:fromData PressureDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler, OptimizationEnabler
]

instance VibrationData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Vibration data with temporal aggregation for mechanical health assessment and failure prediction."
    DTDFVocab:fromData VibrationDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler, ControlEnabler
]

instance ContextualData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Semantically enriched contextual data combining time-series sensor data with domain-specific ontologies for unified analysis."
    DTDFVocab:fromData ContextualDataTraces
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowEnabler, PredictionEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance SysMLEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML-based modeling toolchain enabling functional and logical architecture modeling with Internal Block Diagrams and state machines."
    DTDFVocab:enables FunctionalAnalysisService, BehavioralModelingService, VisualizationService
]

instance MontiArcEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc component-based modeling framework supporting automata-based behavior specification and compositional analysis."
    DTDFVocab:enables BehavioralModelingService, ControlService, OptimizationService
]

instance SimulinkEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink simulation engine for dynamic system modeling, control design, and real-time execution of behavioral models."
    DTDFVocab:enables SimulationService, OptimizationService, ControlService
]

instance CppEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [

    DTDFVocab:enables ControlService, DataProcessingService
]

instance DataAggregationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Middleware component processing one-way data flows from physical systems with ETL pipelines and schema-on-read approaches."

]

instance DigitalShadowEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Digital shadow integration layer aggregating and abstracting contextual data traces for purpose-specific analysis."
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService, PredictionService
]

instance PredictionEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Time-series analysis and behavioral modeling engine for prognostic modeling and failure risk forecasting."

]

instance ControlEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [

]

instance OptimizationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Simulation-based optimization toolchain for parameter tuning and scenario testing without physical intervention."

]

instance VisualizationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Interactive 3D/4D modeling and dashboarding platform for real-time KPI visualization and comparative analytics."

]

// C6: Services
instance MonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time data aggregation and abstraction service providing diagnostic insights about component health and operational anomalies."
    DTDFVocab:provides ComponentHealthInsight, OperationalAnomalyInsight
]

    DTDFVocab:provides StatePredictionInsight, FailureModeInsight
]

instance PredictionService : DTDFVocab:Service [

    DTDFVocab:provides EfficiencyOptimizationInsight, CostReductionInsight

instance VisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in Digital Twin systems defines a **bidirectional control loop** where the digital model influences physical operations through validated commands. Key technical aspects include:

1. **Command Types**:
   - **Reactive controls** (e.g., **PID controllers**, **fuzzy logic rules**) for real-time adjustments (e.g., motor speed, temperature regulation).
   - **Predictive commands** (e.g., **reinforcement learning-based optimizations**) for proactive system adjustments (e.g., predictive maintenance scheduling).
   - **Configuration updates** (e.g., firmware patches, parameter tuning) via **over-the-air (OTA) deployment** mechanisms.

2. **Control Message Formats**:
   - **Structured protocols** like **OPC UA Information Models** for standardized command encoding.
   - **JSON-based APIs** (e.g., **RESTful endpoints**) for human-readable control interfaces (e.g., **Swagger/OpenAPI** specifications).
   - **Binary protocols** (e.g., **Protocol Buffers**) for low-latency, high-throughput command execution (e.g., in robotics).

3. **Validation and Authentication**:
   - **Pre-command validation** via **model-driven verification** (e.g., **SysML state machines**, **Finite State Machines**) to ensure logical consistency.
   - **Cryptographic authentication** (e.g., **TLS 1.3**, **JWT tokens**) to prevent unauthorized command injection.
   - **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** to restrict command execution to authorized entities (e.g., **OAuth 2.0** for multi-tenant systems).

4. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Closed-loop control** with **real-time feedback** from physical sensors (e.g., **cycle time measurements**, **force/torque feedback** in robotics).
   - **Adaptive learning** where the DT adjusts control parameters dynamically based on **reinforcement learning** or **Bayesian optimization**.

5. **Conflict Resolution**:
   - **Priority-based arbitration** (e.g., **Ethernet/IP** or **PROFINET** for industrial networks) to resolve conflicting commands from multiple DT instances.
   - **Consensus algorithms** (e.g., **Raft**, **Paxos**) in distributed DT deployments to synchronize control decisions across edge nodes.

6. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - **Acknowledgment protocols** (e.g., **ACK/NACK messages**) to confirm receipt and execution status.
   - **Audit logging** (e.g., **ELK Stack**) to trace command lineage and system state changes for post-mortem analysis.
   - **Rollback mechanisms** (e.g., **transactional databases**) to revert to a stable state if command execution fails.

*Note*: Active components in the DT (e.g., **simulation engines**, **AI agents**) must integrate with **real-time operating systems (RTOS)** or **industrial PLCs** to ensure deterministic control response times (<10ms for critical applications).
"
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's temporal aspects are governed by the synchronization requirements between physical and digital components, which are implicitly derived from Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies such as SysML, ADLs (e.g., AADL, ACME), and simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink. While explicit synchronization frequencies or latency requirements are not detailed in the provided documents, the context implies that **real-time or near-real-time synchronization** is critical for active Digital Twin components (as opposed to passive digital shadows). The temporal granularity depends on the abstraction layers (requirements, functional/logical architecture, physical layer) and may vary from **millisecond-level updates** for control systems (e.g., embedded logic in MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines) to **second/minute-level aggregations** for higher-level analytics. Latency constraints are implicitly tied to the integration of simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) and general-purpose languages (C++, Java), where **sub-second latency** is often assumed for closed-loop feedback mechanisms. The documents do not specify exact frequencies but emphasize that **temporal alignment** between physical and digital representations is a core requirement for active Digital Twin operation, distinguishing it from one-way digital shadow data flows."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's orchestration follows **architecture-centric patterns** rooted in MBSE methodologies, where **component-based decomposition** (via ADLs like AADL or ACME) and **behavioral modeling** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton) define the core constellation. Integration of **heterogeneous tools** (e.g., Simulink for detailed behavior, general-purpose languages like C++/Java for execution) introduces a **modular, tool-chain-based architecture**, where each tool contributes to specific layers (e.g., logical architecture in MontiArc, physical layer in Simulink). Resource management is implicitly handled through **tool-specific mechanisms** (e.g., Simulink’s real-time execution environments) and **ADL-defined connectors**, which enforce data consistency across components. System-wide coordination is achieved through **abstraction layer alignment** (e.g., RFLP methodology’s sequential refinement from requirements to physics) and **stakeholder-specific perspectives** (e.g., integrating SysML models with enterprise systems via interoperability standards). The documents do not specify explicit orchestration frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes, microservices) but highlight that **active Digital Twin components** (unlike passive shadows) require **closed-loop feedback**, necessitating **real-time data processing pipelines** and **cross-tool synchronization**."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing **four primary abstraction layers**: *requirements*, *functional architecture*, *logical architecture*, and *product/technical layer*. These layers follow a **hierarchical decomposition** approach, where each stage transitions via defined methodologies such as **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**. The lifecycle begins with **requirements modeling** (e.g., SysML diagrams) to define system objectives, progresses to **functional architecture** (e.g., SysML Internal Block Diagrams, IBDs) describing high-level functions, then to **logical architecture** (e.g., **AADL, ACME, or MontiArc**) detailing component-connector interactions and interfaces, and culminates in the **product/technical layer** where **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, C++, or Java)** implement behavioral models. Transition management between stages relies on **architecture description languages (ADLs)** and **state machines** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 statecharts) to ensure consistency. Lifecycle governance is implicitly supported by **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** (per [31]), where **model interoperability** across tools (e.g., SysML, Simulink) is critical. No explicit governance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) are mentioned in the document, but the lifecycle inherently adheres to **MBSE best practices** for iterative refinement and validation.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system emphasizes **model fidelity** through multi-layered MBSE methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) to ensure alignment between requirements, functional/logical architectures, and physical/technical layers. **Validation and verification (V&V) methods** include:
- **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** (e.g., SysML IBDs, AADL) for structural consistency checks.
- **Simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation methods [29], [30]) for behavioral validation.
- **Code-based validation** in general-purpose languages (C++, Java) for low-level fidelity.
**Uncertainty sources** are implicitly addressed via data aggregation from digital shadows ([32]), where **error detection** relies on active DT components ([35]–[37]) to interpret discrepancies. **Quality assurance processes** involve cross-tool consistency (e.g., integrating SysML, ADLs, and simulation tools) and stakeholder alignment ([31]). However, **quantitative uncertainty quantification** (e.g., confidence intervals, error bounds) and **correction mechanisms** (e.g., adaptive model updates) are not explicitly detailed in the provided documents."
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **technical network infrastructure** for Digital Twin systems requires a **highly resilient, low-latency architecture** to support bidirectional data flows between physical and virtual domains. Key specifications include:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Industrial-grade protocols**:
     - **OPC UA** (for secure, platform-independent communication between DT and IoT devices).
     - **MQTT/SN** (for lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging in constrained environments like **IIoT edge nodes**).
     - **Ethernet/IP** or **PROFINET** (for deterministic control in manufacturing).
   - **Cloud-native protocols**:
     - **gRPC** (for high-performance microservices communication).
     - **WebSockets** (for real-time bidirectional streams in web-based DT dashboards).

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**:
   - **Hybrid topology**:
     - **Edge computing nodes** (e.g., **NVIDIA Jetson**, **Intel Edge Insights**) for local preprocessing and latency reduction.
     - **Cloud data centers** (e.g., **AWS IoT Greengrass**, **Azure Digital Twins**) for centralized analytics and storage.
     - **On-premise gateways** (e.g., **Siemens MindSphere**, **PTC ThingWorx**) to bridge legacy systems.
   - **Hierarchical routing**:
     - **Multi-tier network segmentation** (e.g., **VLANs**, **SDN controllers**) to isolate critical DT components from non-critical traffic.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
   - **Real-time systems** (<10ms latency):
     - **10Gbps Ethernet** for deterministic control loops (e.g., **TSN - Time-Sensitive Networking**).
     - **5G/6G private networks** (e.g., **Standalone 5G SA**) for mobile DT applications.
   - **Non-real-time analytics** (100ms–1s latency):
     - **1Gbps symmetric connections** for bulk data transfer (e.g., **HDF5**, **Parquet** formats).
     - **Compression algorithms** (e.g., **Zstandard**, **LZ4**) to reduce bandwidth usage by **30–70%** for sensor data.

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
   - **Active-active clustering** (e.g., **Kubernetes**, **Docker Swarm**) for DT service redundancy.
   - **Geo-replicated databases** (e.g., **CockroachDB**, **MongoDB Global Cluster**) to ensure **99.999% uptime**.
   - **Failover protocols** (e.g., **VRRP**, **Keepalived**) for seamless transition during network partitions.
   - **Data replication strategies**:
     - **Synchronous replication** (for critical control data).
     - **Asynchronous replication** (for non-critical analytics).

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **Network-level security**:
     - **IPsec** for encrypted communication between edge and cloud.
     - **TLS 1.3** for end-to-end encryption of DT APIs.
   - **Zero-trust architecture**:
     - **Continuous authentication** (e.g., **device posture checks**, **behavioral biometrics**).
     - **Micro-segmentation** (e.g., **Cisco ACI**, **VMware NSX**) to limit lateral movement.
   - **Anomaly detection**:
     - **Network intrusion detection** (e.g., **Suricata**, **Zeek**) for DT-specific attack patterns (e.g., **twin-jamming**).
     - **AI-driven threat modeling** (e.g., **Darktrace**, **Palo Alto XSOAR**) to detect adversarial DT manipulations.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The **deployment infrastructure** for Digital Twin systems must balance **computational demands**, **scalability**, and **operational resilience** across **cloud**, **edge**, and **on-premise** environments. Key technical requirements include:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Cloud platforms**:
     - **Multi-cloud deployments** (e.g., **AWS IoT Core + Azure Digital Twins**) for vendor agnosticism.
     - **Serverless architectures** (e.g., **AWS Lambda**, **Google Cloud Functions**) for event-driven DT workloads.
   - **Edge platforms**:
     - **NVIDIA EGX** or **AWS IoT Greengrass** for localized DT simulation and inference.
     - **Raspberry Pi clusters** (for low-cost, low-power DT prototypes).
   - **On-premise**:
     - **Virtualized environments** (e.g., **VMware vSphere**, **KVM**) for legacy system integration.
     - **Containerized deployments** (e.g., **Docker**, **Podman**) with **Kubernetes** for orchestration.

2. **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
   - **CPU/GPU resources**:
     - **High-performance computing (HPC)** (e.g., **NVIDIA A100 GPUs**) for physics-based simulations (e.g., **ANSYS Fluent**, **COMSOL**).
     - **FPGA acceleration** (e.g., **Xilinx Alveo**) for real-time signal processing in DT control loops.
   - **Memory**:
     - **16GB–128GB RAM** per DT instance (scaling with model complexity; e.g., **graph neural networks** for large-scale DTs).
   - **Storage**:
     - **Hot storage** (e.g., **NVMe SSDs**) for real-time data (latency <1ms).
     - **Cold storage** (e.g., **S3-compatible object storage**) for historical DT snapshots (compressed via **ZFS** or **Ceph**).
     - **In-memory databases** (e.g., **Redis**, **Memcached**) for sub-millisecond query responses.

3. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **Horizontal scaling**:
     - **Auto-scaling policies** (e.g., **AWS Auto Scaling**, **Kubernetes HPA**) based on **CPU/memory utilization** or **queue depth** (e.g., **Kafka lag**).
   - **Vertical scaling**:
     - **Elastic GPU scaling** (e.g., **NVIDIA vGPU**) for GPU-intensive DT simulations.
   - **Partitioning strategies**:
     - **Spatial partitioning** (e.g., **R-tree indexes**) for geographically distributed DTs.
     - **Temporal partitioning** (e.g., **time-series databases** like **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**) to isolate historical vs. real-time data.

4. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **Monolithic deployments** (e.g., **single-tenant DT applications** in **SAP Digital Twin**).
   - **Microservices architecture** (e.g., **Kubernetes-native DT components** with **service meshes** like **Istio**).
   - **Hybrid cloud-edge deployments** (e.g., **AWS Outposts** for on-premise DT workloads).
   - **Federated learning** (e.g., **TensorFlow Federated**) for privacy-preserving DT training across distributed sites.

5. **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
   - **High availability (HA) clusters**:
     - **Multi-AZ deployments** (e.g., **AWS Multi-AZ RDS**) for database redundancy.
     - **Active-active replication** (e.g., **CockroachDB**) with **RPO < 1s**.
   - **Disaster recovery (DR)**:
     - **Cross-region replication** (e.g., **AWS Global Accelerator**) for DT failover.
     - **Immutable backups** (e.g., **WORM storage**) to prevent tampering.
   - **SLA guarantees**:
     - **99.99% uptime** for critical DT components (e.g., **AWS Service Level Agreements**).
     - **Predictive maintenance** for infrastructure (e.g., **AI-driven capacity planning** via **Gartner’s Digital Twin for IT**).
"
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "Horizontal integration in the Digital Twin system is facilitated through **interoperability standards** and **heterogeneous data exchange protocols** inherent to MBSE and ADL ecosystems. The documents reference **SysML IBDs** (Internal Block Diagrams) and **MontiArc connectors** as mechanisms to integrate disparate components (e.g., physical sensors, simulation tools, or enterprise systems) via **standardized interfaces**. While explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) are not mentioned, the context implies reliance on **tool-specific interoperability** (e.g., Simulink’s compatibility with MATLAB/Simulink Toolboxes for external system integration) and **ADL-defined data contracts** (e.g., AADL’s port and connection models). Dependencies on external systems are addressed through **layered abstraction** (e.g., enterprise systems interacting with the logical/functional layer via SysML models), ensuring **decoupled integration**. The documents do not detail **cross-twin coordination** but suggest that **enterprise system integration** is achieved via **model-driven approaches** (e.g., SPES or SMArDT methodologies) that bridge MBSE artifacts with legacy systems."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The DT system adheres to **industry standards** primarily through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks, including:
- **Modeling Languages**: **SysML v1.7** (OMG standard) for requirements, functional/logical architecture, and behavior (state machines).
- **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **AADL** (SAE AS5506), **ACME** (IEEE P1471), and **MontiArc** (research-based but aligned with ADL principles) for component-connector modeling.
- **Simulation Standards**: **MathWorks Simulink** (IEEE 1541-compliant for model exchange) and **general-purpose languages (C++, Java)** for behavioral implementation.
- **Interoperability**: **Model interchange formats** (e.g., SysML profiles, XMI) enable cross-tool compatibility, though no **specific interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA, ROS 2) are cited. The **Digital Shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) implies **UML class diagrams** for conceptual alignment but does not reference **formal certification frameworks** (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010 for architectures).
No **compliance frameworks** (e.g., IEC 62264 for enterprise integration) or **certification requirements** (e.g., ISO 27001 for data security) are mentioned. The document focuses on **methodological standards** (MBSE, ADLs) rather than regulatory compliance.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates multiple **safety-critical mechanisms** and **cybersecurity protocols** to ensure operational integrity and threat mitigation. The **safety cell** restricts the maximum working range of the first axis to **[-90°, 90°]** and enforces a **maximum angular velocity of |q̇_max| = 75°/s**, preventing unsafe movements. The system employs **dual position encoders** (motor encoder and secondary encoder behind the gearbox) to reduce **backlash and positional uncertainty**, enabling real-time monitoring of drive train elasticity and friction for fault detection.

The robot is controlled via **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl. CNC controller**, which supports **separate axis control** with configurable inputs (position, speed, acceleration) and outputs (target/actual position, derivatives, and motor current). **Motor current (I_M)** is converted into applied torque (τ_M) using **known motor constants**, allowing torque-based monitoring for abnormal conditions.
